title: Vendor Connector Authorization Model Undefined
detail: No concrete authentication/authorization mechanism (e.g., mTLS, OAuth 2.0 client credentials, API keys) is specified for Philips IntelliVue and GE CARESCAPE inbound connections, leaving the trust boundary undefined.
prd_quote: Authorization model and egress policy for vendor connectors are TBD — some teams have been connecting to unapproved external tools without formal review.
gap_description: No concrete authentication/authorization mechanism (e.g., mTLS, OAuth 2.0 client credentials, API keys) is specified for Philips IntelliVue and GE CARESCAPE inbound connections, leaving the trust boundary undefined.

options.label: Mutual TLS + OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials per Vendor
options.resolution: Each approved vendor connector must authenticate via mTLS with a vendor-issued certificate AND present a short-lived OAuth 2.0 client-credentials token scoped to 'vitals:ingest'. Certificates must be rotated every 90 days and registered in the internal service catalog before activation.
options.rationale: HIPAA §164.312(d) requires entity authentication; mTLS satisfies technical safeguard requirements and OAuth scoping enforces minimum-necessary access per §164.514(b).
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design a certificate authority integration and token introspection endpoint; CodeGen must implement mTLS handshake and token validation middleware for the WebSocket upgrade path.

options.label: API Key per Vendor with IP Allowlist
options.resolution: Each vendor is issued a static API key transmitted in a custom header; inbound connections are additionally restricted to a pre-approved IP CIDR range registered in the firewall policy.
options.rationale: Lower implementation complexity and compatible with vendor SDKs that do not support OAuth; acceptable under HIPAA §164.312(d) if key rotation and audit logging are enforced.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design key-rotation workflow and IP allowlist management; CodeGen implements header extraction and IP validation middleware, but loses fine-grained scope control.

title: Egress PHI Redaction Scope Not Defined
detail: The PRD states 'PII redaction at egress' but does not enumerate which FHIR Observation fields constitute PHI/PII that must be stripped or masked before events reach the clinical event bus consumer.
gap_description: The PRD states 'PII redaction at egress' but does not enumerate which FHIR Observation fields constitute PHI/PII that must be stripped or masked before events reach the clinical event bus consumer.

options.label: Enumerate FHIR Fields Against HIPAA 18 Identifiers
options.resolution: Define a canonical redaction manifest: subject.reference (patient ID), performer, encounter.reference, and device.identifier must be tokenized using a HMAC-SHA256 pseudonym before egress. All 18 HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers per §164.514(b)(2) must be evaluated against every FHIR Observation field and documented in the data dictionary.
options.rationale: HIPAA §164.514(b)(2) Safe Harbor method requires removal of all 18 enumerated identifiers; explicit field mapping prevents accidental PHI leakage to the Cardiology event bus.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design a tokenization service; CodeGen must implement a FHIR field-level redaction filter applied at the egress serialization layer before publishing to the event bus.

options.label: Pass Full FHIR Resource with Audit Log
options.resolution: Transmit full FHIR Observation resources to the event bus without field redaction, relying on the event bus's existing access controls and audit logging to satisfy minimum-necessary requirements.
options.rationale: Cardiology team as the designated consumer may have a legitimate need for full patient context; HIPAA §164.502(b) minimum-necessary can be satisfied by role-based access rather than data stripping.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must ensure event bus enforces consumer-level ACLs; CodeGen skips redaction middleware but must instrument comprehensive audit logging per §164.312(b).

title: PHI Data Retention Period Not Defined
detail: The PRD acknowledges PHI in transit but does not specify how long raw ingest payloads, normalized FHIR events, or WebSocket session logs containing PHI are retained before deletion or archival.
gap_description: The PRD acknowledges PHI in transit but does not specify how long raw ingest payloads, normalized FHIR events, or WebSocket session logs containing PHI are retained before deletion or archival.

options.label: 6-Year Retention with Encrypted Archival and Automated Deletion
options.resolution: All PHI-bearing ingest payloads and FHIR Observation events are retained for exactly 6 years from creation date in AES-256 encrypted cold storage, then automatically deleted via a scheduled purge job. WebSocket session logs are retained for 90 days then purged. Retention schedule must be documented in the organization's HIPAA policies.
options.rationale: HIPAA §164.530(j) requires covered entities to retain documentation for 6 years; automated deletion minimizes breach exposure surface and satisfies minimum-necessary per §164.514(b).
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design tiered storage (hot→cold→delete) with lifecycle policies; CodeGen must implement a retention-tagging mechanism on every stored record and a purge job with audit logging.

options.label: 90-Day Hot Retention, No Archival
options.resolution: PHI-bearing events are retained in hot storage for 90 days then permanently deleted. No cold archival tier is implemented; downstream consumers are responsible for their own retention.
options.rationale: Minimizes PHI exposure window and storage cost; appropriate if the clinical event bus consumers (Cardiology) own long-term retention and this API is treated as a transient ingest pipeline.
options.downstream_impact: Architect designs simple TTL-based deletion; CodeGen implements TTL tagging only, but the organization must document that downstream consumers satisfy the 6-year HIPAA retention requirement independently.

title: WebSocket Session Authentication Mechanism Not Specified
detail: The PRD specifies WebSocket as the transport but does not define how WebSocket connections are authenticated and re-authenticated during long-lived sessions carrying PHI.
gap_description: The PRD specifies WebSocket as the transport but does not define how WebSocket connections are authenticated and re-authenticated during long-lived sessions carrying PHI.

options.label: JWT Bearer Token at Upgrade with 15-Minute Refresh via Sub-Protocol
options.resolution: WebSocket connections must present a signed JWT (RS256, 15-minute expiry) in the HTTP Upgrade request Authorization header. Clients must refresh tokens via a dedicated WebSocket sub-protocol message before expiry; the server must terminate connections where the token has been expired for >30 seconds without refresh.
options.rationale: HIPAA §164.312(d) requires automatic logoff; 15-minute token expiry with forced refresh enforces session re-authentication and limits PHI exposure from abandoned connections per §164.312(a)(2)(iii).
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design a token refresh sub-protocol and connection termination policy; CodeGen must implement JWT validation on upgrade, a server-side token expiry watchdog, and client-side refresh logic.

options.label: One-Time Token at Upgrade, No Mid-Session Re-Auth
options.resolution: A one-time signed token is validated at WebSocket upgrade; the connection remains authenticated for its lifetime without mid-session re-authentication, relying on TLS for ongoing session integrity.
options.rationale: Simpler implementation; acceptable for short-lived connections where session duration is bounded by the bedside monitor's reconnect interval (typically <5 minutes).
options.downstream_impact: CodeGen implements token validation only at upgrade; Architect must document maximum permissible session duration and enforce it via a server-side connection timeout, but loses fine-grained re-auth control.

title: 99.95% Uptime Measurement Window Not Specified
detail: The SLA states 99.95% uptime but does not define the measurement window (monthly vs. annual), exclusion criteria (planned maintenance), or the error budget enforcement mechanism.
gap_description: The SLA states 99.95% uptime but does not define the measurement window (monthly vs. annual), exclusion criteria (planned maintenance), or the error budget enforcement mechanism.

options.label: Rolling 30-Day Window with Planned Maintenance Exclusion
options.resolution: 99.95% uptime is measured over a rolling 30-day calendar window (~21.9 minutes allowable downtime/month). Planned maintenance windows pre-announced ≥72 hours in advance and not exceeding 60 minutes/month are excluded from the calculation. Breaches trigger an automated PagerDuty P1 incident.
options.rationale: A 30-day rolling window is standard in healthcare SaaS contracts and aligns with NIST SP 800-53 AU-12 continuous monitoring; monthly granularity enables faster SLA breach detection than annual.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design uptime monitoring with rolling-window aggregation; CodeGen must instrument health-check endpoints and integrate with the alerting pipeline to surface breach conditions.

options.label: Annual Calendar Window
options.resolution: 99.95% uptime is measured over a January–December calendar year (~4.38 hours allowable downtime/year), with no planned maintenance exclusions.
options.rationale: Annual windows are simpler to report and may align with existing vendor SLA contracts for Philips and GE integrations.
options.downstream_impact: Architect designs annual aggregation dashboards; CodeGen does not need rolling-window logic, but the system tolerates longer short-term outages without triggering alerts.

title: Sub-100ms Latency Measurement Point Ambiguous
detail: The PRD does not define where the 100ms latency is measured — whether from bedside monitor transmission, WebSocket frame receipt, or event bus publish confirmation — making the SLA unverifiable.
gap_description: The PRD does not define where the 100ms latency is measured — whether from bedside monitor transmission, WebSocket frame receipt, or event bus publish confirmation — making the SLA unverifiable.

options.label: Define Latency as WebSocket Frame Receipt to Event Bus Publish ACK
options.resolution: Ingest latency is defined as the p99 elapsed time from WebSocket frame receipt at the API gateway to acknowledgment of publish from the clinical event bus broker, measured at 1-minute intervals. The <100ms threshold applies at p99 over a rolling 5-minute window.
options.rationale: Measuring at p99 from gateway ingress to broker ACK captures the full system-controlled path; p99 is the standard SRE threshold per Google SRE Book and avoids outlier distortion from network jitter outside system control.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must instrument distributed tracing spans at gateway ingress and broker publish; CodeGen must emit OpenTelemetry trace IDs on every WebSocket frame to enable latency attribution.

options.label: End-to-End from Bedside Monitor to Event Bus
options.resolution: Ingest latency is measured end-to-end from the timestamp embedded in the vendor device payload to event bus publish ACK, with <100ms as the p95 threshold.
options.rationale: Captures true clinical data freshness including vendor-side transmission delay, which is relevant for patient safety decisions by the Cardiology consumer.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must design clock-synchronization (NTP/PTP) between bedside monitors and the API gateway; CodeGen must parse and validate device-embedded timestamps, adding complexity for Philips/GE payload normalization.

title: FHIR Observation Resource Identifier Format Not Specified
detail: The PRD does not specify the FHIR Observation resource identifier scheme (e.g., UUID v4, OID, vendor-native ID passthrough), creating a risk of ID collisions across Philips and GE device streams.
prd_quote: HL7 FHIR Observation resources for heart rate, SpO2, blood pressure, temperature.
gap_description: The PRD does not specify the FHIR Observation resource identifier scheme (e.g., UUID v4, OID, vendor-native ID passthrough), creating a risk of ID collisions across Philips and GE device streams.

options.label: System-Generated UUID v4 with Vendor Source Identifier in Meta
options.resolution: The ingest service assigns a UUID v4 as the FHIR Observation.id for every normalized event. The original vendor-native device identifier is preserved in Observation.meta.source as a URN (e.g., urn:philips:intellivue:<device-id>:<seq>). This is mandatory for all inbound vendor streams.
options.rationale: UUID v4 guarantees global uniqueness across multi-vendor streams per FHIR R4 §2.1.0.1; preserving vendor IDs in meta enables audit traceability without polluting the primary identifier namespace.
options.downstream_impact: CodeGen must implement a UUID generation step in the normalization pipeline and a meta.source mapping table per vendor; Architect must ensure the event bus schema enforces the meta.source URN format.

options.label: Vendor-Native ID Passthrough as FHIR Observation.id
options.resolution: The vendor-supplied device sequence identifier is used directly as Observation.id, prefixed with a vendor namespace (e.g., 'philips-' or 'ge-') to prevent collisions.
options.rationale: Preserves traceability to source device without an additional ID mapping layer; simpler for initial integration with Philips and GE SDKs.
options.downstream_impact: CodeGen must implement vendor-specific ID parsing and prefix logic; Architect must validate that vendor ID formats comply with FHIR id datatype constraints (max 64 chars, regex [A-Za-z0-9\-\.]{1,64}).

title: Clinical Event Bus Naming Convention for Vital Sign Topics Not Defined
detail: The PRD does not define the topic/channel naming convention for the clinical event bus, risking inconsistent topic names across heart rate, SpO2, blood pressure, and temperature streams that the Cardiology consumer must subscribe to.
prd_quote: forward normalized events to a clinical event bus. Cardiology team owns the consumer.
gap_description: The PRD does not define the topic/channel naming convention for the clinical event bus, risking inconsistent topic names across heart rate, SpO2, blood pressure, and temperature streams that the Cardiology consumer must subscribe to.

options.label: Hierarchical Topic Scheme: vitals/{vendor}/{observation-code}
options.resolution: Event bus topics must follow the pattern: vitals/{vendor}/{loinc-code} (e.g., vitals/philips/8867-4 for heart rate, vitals/ge/59408-5 for SpO2). Vendor slug must be lowercase alphanumeric. LOINC codes are mandatory; SNOMED aliases are permitted as secondary routing keys only.
options.rationale: Using LOINC codes as topic identifiers aligns with FHIR R4 Observation.code requirements and enables interoperability with future consumers beyond Cardiology without topic proliferation.
options.downstream_impact: Architect must publish a topic registry with all four vital sign LOINC codes per vendor; CodeGen must implement topic construction logic in the normalization pipeline and validate against the registry at startup.

options.label: Flat Topic Scheme: vitals.{observation-name}
options.resolution: Event bus topics use a flat human-readable scheme: vitals.heart-rate, vitals.spo2, vitals.blood-pressure, vitals.temperature. Vendor origin is encoded in the event payload, not the topic name.
options.rationale: Simpler for the Cardiology consumer to subscribe to without knowledge of LOINC codes or vendor slugs; reduces topic count from 8 (4 vitals × 2 vendors) to 4.
options.downstream_impact: CodeGen implements simpler topic routing but Cardiology consumer must filter by vendor in payload; Architect must ensure the event schema mandates a vendor field to preserve source traceability.
